issue_title: General: hide the return value if it is operation_status_response
issue_body:
(For tracking, this might need some cooperation from autorest) 
With the integration with the latest compute SDK, a few compute operations (`deallocate`, `start`, `stop`, etc) start to return `operation_status_response` objects 
```json
{
  "endTime": "2017-02-08T18:02:31.477182+00:00",
  "error": null,
  "name": "02bdfc3f-6236-4064-9894-15b89ad6b70d",
  "startTime": "2017-02-08T18:02:20.070584+00:00",
  "status": "Succeeded"
}
```
This output is not interesting, because we will get `CloudError` on any error causes. I would very much like the old silent behavior on success

It is possible to hide it by using the transformation. The problem is this response type is per library, not a general type from the auto run time, so the [detection code](https://github.com/yugangw-msft/azure-cli/commit/e91b108d22fd2caf47e1d989943d1a091e23dcb2#diff-08bf3d69436b952a8bf0b428f808dc01R92) at CLI would become pretty hacky, and each command module will need same piece of code to deal with this.

I will open a bug at autorest, that we should at least move the type to common runtime, so cli will handle it at core layer

issue_body:
Hello,

A very simple piece of server code that used to work just fine before now crashes completely (even serving static html content on / route) with the following exception:

```
--- Logging error ---
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/logging/__init__.py", line 978, in emit

    for value, tb in values:

    context = exc.__context__
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute '__context__'
Call stack:

    loop.run_forever()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/asyncio/base_events.py", line 331, in run_forever
    self._run_once()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/asyncio/base_events.py", line 1295, in _run_once
    handle._run()

    self._callback(*self._args)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/asyncio/tasks.py", line 240, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)

    resp = self.handle_error(request, status, exc, message)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/aiohttp/web_protocol.py", line 512, in handle_error

    self.logger.exception(*args, **kw)

```
Forget the logging_error part, it's related to y Python 3.4 bug I guess:
https://github.com/getlogbook/logbook/issues/146

But if you're interested in, I workarounded the issue like this in my asyncio error handler:
```
        if 'exception' in context:
            # Workaround some weird Python bug I guess
            # IE: https://github.com/getlogbook/logbook/issues/146
            exc_info = sys.exc_info()
            if exc_info is not None and exc_info != (None, None, None):
                logger.exception("Uncatched exception occurred in asyncio loop: %r", context['exception'])
            else:
                logger.error("Uncatched exception occurred in asyncio loop: %r", context['exception'])
        else:
            logger.error("Uncatched exception occurred in asyncio loop: %r", context['message'])
```
Thanks in advance for your help, I have absolutely no clue of what's going on...

Best regards, Adam.

Compared to what knitr produces for tables, the tables in the notebook look horrible:

e.g. pander tables in an R markdown document converted to html:
![2016-03-08_134713](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/890156/13602029/4d9c6186-e534-11e5-8b52-7fd01b8742c7.png)

compared to similar code (the notebook doesn't understand the pandoc markdown table syntax :-( and therefore the pandoc step to convert it to html) in the jupyter notebook:

![2016-03-08_135209](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/890156/13602117/fc0ce042-e534-11e5-9002-47acff1414ce.png)

I also tried to include bootstrap styling (which knitr used for html), but this does not seem to work, as it seems the default styling overwrites it(?):

```R
library(pander)
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(IRdisplay)
panderOptions("table.split.table", 200)
nice_pander <- function(...){
    t = capture.output(pander(...))
    mdFile <- tempfile(fileext=".md")
    cat(paste(t, collapse="\n"), file=mdFile)
    htmlFile <- knitr::pandoc(input = mdFile, format = "html")
    t = readr::read_file(htmlFile)
    #print(t)
    display_html(paste(t, collapse="\n"))
   display_html("<link rel='stylesheet' href='http://maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com/bootstrap/3.3.6/css/bootstrap.min.css'>
<script src='http://maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com/bootstrap/3.3.6/js/bootstrap.min.js'></script>
<script>$('tr.header').parent('thead').parent('table').addClass('table table-condensed');</script>
")
}
df <- data.frame(a=0:20,

nice_pander(df %>% group_by(c) %>% do(tidy(t.test(a~b, data = .))))

issue_title: Create transaction broadcast service
issue_body:
Registering Blockstack names requires broadcasting a number of bitcoin transactions and waiting between them. Transactions need to be broadcast within certain intervals, otherwise the name registration will fail.

This is fine when a registration is performed on a server which is always running and connected to the network.

It poses a problem for registrations conducted by end users using Blockstack Portal because most of them use machines that are not always on and many use laptops which can have limited power and intermittent network connectivity. 

Name registration will fail if the user is unable to broadcast the transactions at the right time.

This can happen in several scenarios:
1. The user turns her computer off after submitting the registration and doesn't turn it back on and restart Blockstack within a certain window of time (~12 hours)
2. The user's computer loses network connectivity and is unable to broadcast the transactions at the right time.

One way to solve this is to create an optional service where a user can generate transactions in advance and send them to a trusted 3rd broadcast service that queues them up and sends them out at the appropriate time.

We'd make this on by default in Blockstack for Mac and likely other end user platforms. Advanced users should be able to disable this feature in which their local machine would be responsible for broadcasting the transactions at the proper time. They should also be able to point it to alternative deployments of the service.

There should be two API endpoints: 1 for submitting the transactions and another for checking their status in the broadcast queue.

Tagging @muneeb-ali @shea256 @kantai @jcnelson for additional input.

issue_title: Timeout value for sync request
issue_body:
According [Requests doc](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#timeouts) it accept timeout parameter in **seconds**.
But in [api.py#L78](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/blob/master/matrix_client/api.py#L78) to this parameter passed huge value **30000 sec = 8.3 hours** from [api.py#L63](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/blob/master/matrix_client/api.py#L63)
I think line [api.py#L78](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/blob/master/matrix_client/api.py#L78)  must be like: `"timeout": timeout_ms/1000.0`

issue_title: pytest-xdist hangs slaves crash during collection
issue_body:
```python
# contents of test_foo.py
import os
os._exit(1)
```

```
$ py.test -n2 test_foo.py
...
gw0 ok / gw1 C[gw0] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw0
gw2 C / gw1 ok[gw1] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw1
gw2 ok / gw3 C[gw2] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw2
gw4 C / gw3 ok[gw3] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw3
gw4 ok / gw5 C[gw4] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw4
gw6 C / gw5 ok[gw5] node down: Not properly terminated
...
```
Here pytest-xdist will be stuck trying to restart slaves forever.

By using `--max-slave-restart` it reaches the maximum but hangs anyway:

```
$ py.test -n2 test_foo.py --max-slave-restart=2
...
gw0 ok / gw1 C[gw0] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw0
gw2 C / gw1 ok[gw1] node down: Not properly terminated
Replacing crashed slave gw1
gw2 ok / gw3 C[gw2] node down: Not properly terminated
Maximum crashed slaves reached: 2
gw2 ok / gw3 ok[gw3] node down: Not properly terminated
Maximum crashed slaves reached: 2
(hangs)
```

This is a minimum example of an issue that came up at work, where one of the main modules of an application was causing a core dump on importing.

cc @gqmelo

issue_title: The --boxed option can fail to isolate tests with threads
issue_body:
I believe that the --boxed option can fail for tests which spawn threads. I don't have a great demo of this bug but am experiencing this in my code:

  1. If I run `py.test --boxed /path/to/X /path/to/X` everything is fine because of the boxing.
  2. If I run `py.test /path/to/X /path/to/X` I get test failures due to lack of boxing and thread confusion.
  3. If I run `py.test --boxed .` in the top-level directory file X fails. 

Is it possible that the --boxed behavior is different if I explicitly specify the files vs run it on `.`?

I suspect that using `os.fork` is not truly providing test isolation because in some cases threads, locks, daemon threads, or some other thread stuff is leaking across the `os.fork` call.

It would be great if `--boxed` was implemented using the subprocess module to start a truly isolated process. This would also have the advantage of working on windows. 

My guess is that there are reasons that hasn't been done. So if boxing is going to continue to use fork, it would be great if there was a way to understand why test 1 above works while test 3 fails.

Thanks.

issue_title: Keeping some survivors between generations
issue_body:
Hi, in my personal version of CmaEs I tried to keep samples longer than for a single generation. Typically, the fitness computation will take a significant amount of time so if we can re-use it then that's great.

If I keep all of one generation into the next generation and keep the top X% by fitness, convergence suffers. But if I only let them stay around for a single extra generation, convergence picks up and it's for free.

Running Rosenbrock with 8 dimensions over 1000 runs I get these results

*with* keep survivors
1000: t=18.9ms, evals=4195.2, hits=100.00 %

*without* keep survivors
1000: t=20.9ms, evals=4870.7, hits=100.00 %

Meaning a 13+% of evaluation reduction and both converge 100% of the time.

any thoughts,
mattias

issue_title: [SDK 2.1.300] Command 'dotnet publish' fails for projects with GeneratePackageOnBuild=true
issue_body:
Command 'dotnet publish' fails for projects publishing with GeneratePackageOnBuild=true. 
TargetFramework is netcoreapp2.0.

`<PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>netcoreapp2.0</TargetFramework>
    <GeneratePackageOnBuild>true</GeneratePackageOnBuild>
  </PropertyGroup>`

This used to work with the previous SDK version 2.1.101. 
The error it generates is:
"C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\2.1.300\Sdks\Microsoft.NET.Sdk\targets\Microsoft.NET.Publish.targets(168,5): error MSB3030: Could not copy the file "obj\Debug\netcoreapp2.0\<anyAssemblyName>.dll" because it was not found. "

Workaround: build first, and then publish.

issue_title: _set_by_cli sometimes spuriously returns True
issue_body:
In new code intended to fix #2503, I use `_set_by_cli` as one criterion for deciding to save something in the renewal config file. I found that it sometimes returns `True`, resulting in saving items, even when I did not actually set those things from the CLI at all. The items in question that I observed so far were these:

```
apache_enmod = a2enmod
apache_server_root = /etc/apache2
apache_dismod = a2dismod
apache_handle_sites = True
apache_handle_modules = True
standalone_supported_challenges = "tls-sni-01,http-01"
apache_le_vhost_ext = -le-ssl.conf
apache_challenge_location = /etc/apache2
nginx_server_root = /etc/nginx
nginx_ctl = nginx
apache_vhost_root = /etc/apache2/sites-available
```

There could be others, but these were ones I saw so far.

Cc @pde

issue_title: Errors on subsequent runs if /etc/letsencrypt or /var/lib/letsencrypt are created with a conservative umask
issue_body:
I ran into a problem with `sudo venv/bin/letsencrypt --authenticator apache auth` or similar after the first time I run it (from the letsencrypt.log file):
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "venv/bin/letsencrypt", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('letsencrypt==0.1', 'console_scripts', 'letsencrypt')()
  File "/home/aetherkn/src/letsencrypt/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/cli.py", line 736, in main
    directory, constants.CONFIG_DIRS_MODE, os.geteuid())
  File "/home/aetherkn/src/letsencrypt/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/le_util.py", line 94, in make_or_verify_dir
    "%s exists, this client can't access it" % directory)
Error: /etc/letsencrypt exists, this client can't access it
```
This surprised me because it successfully created both /etc/letsencrypt and /var/lib/letsencrypt after the first run. However, upon investigating, I noticed that the default file mask for creating the directories by `make_or_verify_dir()`  was 0755:

```
def make_or_verify_dir(directory, mode=0o755, uid=0):
  ...
```
And the directories were being created with 0700:
```
$ ls -ld /etc/letsencrypt /var/lib/letsencrypt
drwx------ 5 root root 4096 Sep  9 22:03 /etc/letsencrypt
drwx------ 2 root root 4096 Sep  9 21:58 /var/lib/letsencrypt
```
This caused `check_permissions()` to return false because the permissions were not exactly 0755. In my standard bash configuration I use a umask of 0077 in order to default to denying group and other users form having any permissions on newly created files, and sudo inherits my umask. However, `make_or_verify_dir()` and `check_permissions()` do not take this into account when checking to ensure that the main directories are properly configured.

I would suggest modifying the permissions check to ensure that other users cannot modify the directories, instead of checking for an exact file mode number.

issue_title: TopicPartition_str0 broken on Mac OS X
issue_body:
This one is minor but annoying...

On Mac OS X 10.10.5, Python 2.7.9:
```
>>> from confluent_kafka import TopicPartition
>>> print TopicPartition('topic')
TopicPartition{topic=topic,partition=-1,offset=%lld,error=%s}
```

On Ubuntu Python 2.7.6
```
>>> from confluent_kafka import TopicPartition
>>> print TopicPartition('topic')
TopicPartition{topic=topic,partition=-1,offset=-1001,error=None}

```

I tried to debug this a little and the issue seems to be that mac generates "%lld" and ubuntu generates "%ld" from "PRId64".  PyUnicode_FromFormat (v2) does not support "%lld" (was added in v3.2)

```
mac$ /usr/bin/clang -E -fno-strict-aliasing -fno-common -dynamic -arch x86_64 -g -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/include/python2.7 -c confluent_kafka/src/confluent_kafka.c |grep "TopicPartition{"
 ret = PyUnicodeUCS2_FromFormat("TopicPartition{topic=%s,partition=%""d" ",offset=%""ll" "d"",error=%s}", self->topic, self->partition, self->offset, errstr ? PyString_AsString(PyUnicodeUCS2_AsUTF8String(errstr)) : "None");

ubuntu$ x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc -E -pthread -fno-strict-aliasing -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O2 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -fPIC -I/usr/include/python2.7 -c confluent_kafka/src/confluent_kafka.c|grep "TopicPartition{"
 ret = PyUnicodeUCS4_FromFormat("TopicPartition{topic=%s,partition=%""d" ",offset=%""l" "d"",error=%s}", self->topic, self->partition, self->offset, errstr ? PyString_AsString(PyUnicodeUCS4_AsUTF8String(errstr)) : "None")

```

issue_title: Provide hash in the link for local packages
issue_body:
pip 1.5 and greater would like secure links by default, which by implementation means the link provides a hash of the package in the url. This provides some form of verification of correct file download at least.

You can see some of the [api docs at warehouse](http://warehouse.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api-reference/legacy/#get--simple--project--) how this is done.

It would be great for pypiserver's links to provide this for pip; I believe this would mean you no longer have to pass `--allow-insecure`.

issue_title: Cross Site Scripting Vulnerability
issue_body:
Versions up to and including 1.1.7 contain a cross site scripting vulnerability.  I acknowledge this is not likely to ever be a problem since the normal usage of the server is from pip rather than a web browser.  I only note it because my server was flagged as vulnerable by a Nessus scan.

The vulnerability can be demonstrated by entering the following in a web browser that does **not** filter XSS (on modern browsers, you'll probably have to disable automatic XSS filtering to see the exploit)
`http://my.pypi-server.com:8080/?<script>alert("Vulnerable!");</script>`

If successful, this should pop up an alert window,

issue_title: Parameter with a valueFrom field, type array and no prefix doesn't lead to execution of the expected command
issue_body:
If you use the valueFrom field with an argument of array type and no prefix field, the executed command is the evaluated valueFrom field followed by the array arguments, not just the  evaluated valueFrom field. This is not what I would expect from the cwl specification and behaviour of cwltool.

## Expected Behaviour

From http://www.commonwl.org/v1.0/CommandLineTool.html#CommandLineBinding:
> If valueFrom is a constant string value, use this as the value and apply the binding rules above.

It is not specified in the specification what the binding rules should be if no prefix is defined, but in cwltool if no `prefix` field is present, then cwltool doesn't evaulate the anything for that argument e.g.:
```yaml
cwlVersion: v1.0
inputs:
  - id: parameter
    type:
      - type: array
        items: string
outputs: []
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
```
```yaml
parameter:
  - "item1"
  - "item2"
```

Outputs:
```
/usr/local/bin/cwltool 1.0.20171107133715
Resolved 'test.cwl' to '...'
[job test.cwl] /private/var/folders/5p/p6z8hhy174q_ctt3pn7mx5_c000h1t/T/tmp1mivk5pg$ echo

[job test.cwl] completed success
{}
Final process status is success
```
I would therefore infer that using the workflow and inputs below should execute ` 'echo' 'replacementValue'`, but cwltool executes: `'echo' 'replacementValue' 'item1' 'item2'`.
## Actual Behaviour
Using the workflow and inputs below, cwltool outputs as specified below:

## Workflow Code
### Workflow
```yaml
cwlVersion: v1.0
requirements:
- class: ShellCommandRequirement
- class: InlineJavascriptRequirement
inputs:
  - id: parameter
    type:
      - type: array
        items: string
    inputBinding:
      valueFrom: replacementValue
outputs: []
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
```
### Inputs
```yaml
parameter:
  - "item1"
  - "item2"
```

## Full Traceback
```pytb
/usr/local/bin/cwltool 1.0.20171107133715
Resolved 'test.cwl' to '...'
[job test.cwl] /private/var/folders/5p/p6z8hhy174q_ctt3pn7mx5_c000h1t/T/tmp_jdgzuc4$ /bin/sh \
    -c \
    'echo' 'replacementValue' 'item1' 'item2'
replacementValue item1 item2
[job intervals_test.cwl] completed success
{}
Final process status is success
```

## Your Environment
* cwltool version: 1.0.20171107133715

issue_title: DateField and DateTimeField fields don't honor timezones
issue_body:
I was bitten by this while implementing client-side code: chrome serializes javascript Date objects to '2011-03-30T18:00:00.000Z' format (it converts local time to UTC, the format is valid iso8601). The code reads data from API (it is returned in settings.TIME_ZONE timezone), creates Date object from this data and then eventually sends it back (with representation converted to UTC). Tastypie is somewhere loosing the timezone information during save so the time and the date becomes incorrect. 

I think dates with timezone information should be converted to django's current timezone before writing them to DB (in hydrate method?). 

Sample implementation (introducing pytz dependency):

    import dateutil
    import pytz
    from tastypie import fields
    from django.conf import settings
    
    def string_to_local_datetime(value):
        parsed = dateutil.parser.parse(value)
        try:
            django_tz = pytz.timezone(settings.TIME_ZONE)
            return parsed.astimezone(django_tz)
        except ValueError: # datetime without timezone information
            return parsed
    
    
    class TzDateTimeField(fields.DateTimeField):
        """
        A datetime field that honors timezone information.
        """
        def hydrate(self, bundle):
            value = fields.ApiField.hydrate(self, bundle)
    
            if value and not hasattr(value, 'year'):
                try:
                    # Try to rip a date/datetime out of it.
                    value = string_to_local_datetime(value)
                except ValueError:
                    pass
    
            return value

issue_title: Automatic tool loading
issue_body:
I suggest to load the tools automatically as show in the untested code below. It uses `suffix` and `version` options if present. Custom commands can be injected using the `extra`. We can also move the `GromacsCommandMultiIndex._fake_multi_ndx` logic to a function `merge_ndx()` so users can use it like `grompp(n=merge_ndx('site1.ndx', 'site2.ndx')` in any tool they wants.

```python
def load_v5_tools():
    gmx_cmd = append_suffix('gmx')
    try:
        out = subprocess.check_output([gmx_cmd, '-quiet', 'help', 'commands'])
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        raise GromacsToolLoadingError("Failed to load v5 tools")

    registry = {}
    for line in str(out, 'ascii').splitlines()[5:-2]:
        if line[4] != ' ':
            name = line[4:line.index(' ', 4)]
            fancy = name.replace('-', '_')
            registry[fancy] = type(fancy, (GromacsCommand,),
                                   {'command_name':name})
    return registry


def load_v4_tools():
    tools = ("do_dssp", "editconf", "eneconv", "g_anadock", "g_anaeig",
             "g_analyze", "g_angle", "g_bar", "g_bond", "g_bundle", "g_chi",
             "g_cluster", "g_clustsize", "g_confrms", "g_covar", "g_current",
             "g_density", "g_densmap", "g_densorder", "g_dielectric",
             "g_dipoles", "g_disre", "g_dist", "g_dos", "g_dyecoupl",
             "g_dyndom", "genbox", "genconf", "g_enemat", "g_energy",
             "genion", "genrestr", "g_filter", "g_gyrate", "g_h2order",

issue_title: Progress timer stuck in 'sync' state
issue_body:
The progress timer can sometimes become stuck when playback of one track stops and playback of a new track begins. When that happens, the timer shows '(sync)' and the slider does not move. The problem is more likely to occur when switching from a long duration track to a shorter one.

This is related to how the web client currently makes use of [media-progress-timer](https://github.com/adamcik/media-progress-timer) (also see: https://github.com/adamcik/media-progress-timer/issues/3). Essentially, when the web client polls Mopidy for the time position it sometimes tries to apply the position of the previous track to the timer of the next track.

Possible solutions may be to either:

- rework https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/blob/34e2abf6129fce36547d58e484d875e2b874591a/mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/progress_timer.js#L98-L103 so that we never try to ``set`` the progress timer beyond its current ``duration`` (not sure how to do this as it is unclear exactly when we can expect all of the Mopidy events and track attributes to be in sync when a track change is busy occurring); or
- patch ``timer.js`` so that it does not automatically terminate the update loop when it reaches or exceeds ``duration``. 

I've tried a few changes to resolve this, but event with the most recent attempt (https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/commit/0965f0083e013a11670990f31339f56db366eba1)  this is still an issue.

issue_title: grad_clipping clips gate activations, not gradient
issue_body:
I don't understand the way grad_clipping is implemented in the recurrent layers. Instead of clipping gradients, gate activations get clipped. Is there a reason for this? 1. I don't know any publication doing this and 2. it doesn't solve the exploding gradient problem.

The correct implementation should clip the gradient and it therefore should be implemented in the update functions.

Also you use theano.gradient.grad_clip, which works on each parameter independently (= hard clipping). Consider limiting the L2 norm of the gradient to a certain value, dividing each parameter by the same value and thus preserving the correct directiong of the gradient.
See [On the difficulty of training Recurrent Neural Networks](http://www.jmlr.org/proceedings/papers/v28/pascanu13.pdf), Algorithm 1. 

I use it the following way: 
(copied from https://github.com/kastnerkyle/minet/blob/master/minet/net.py#L236 )
```
def sgd_with_grad_clipping(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate, rescale):
    grads = lasagne.updates.get_or_compute_grads(loss_or_grads, params)
    updates = OrderedDict()

    grad_norm = T.sqrt(sum(map(lambda x: T.sqr(x).sum(), grads)))
    not_finite = T.or_(T.isnan(grad_norm), T.isinf(grad_norm))
    grad_norm = T.sqrt(grad_norm)
    scaling_num = rescale
    scaling_den = T.maximum(rescale, grad_norm)
    for n, (param, grad) in enumerate(zip(params, grads)):
        grad = T.switch(not_finite, 0.1 * param,
            grad * (scaling_num / scaling_den))
        updates[param] = param - learning_rate * grad
    return updates   
```

issue_title: Invalid queries on Django 1.8+
issue_body:
As I've mentioned in #165 there is a bug on Django 1.8+.
Q objects generates different queries then regular kwargs passed to manager.filter() on Django 1.8+
It doesn't add `currency` part to WHERE clause. I've skipped equal parts (Python 2.7, Django 1.9)
```python
>>> print str(ModelWithTwoMoneyFields.objects.filter(Q(amount1__gt=F('amount2'))).query)
... WHERE "testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount1" > ("testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount2")
>>> print str(ModelWithTwoMoneyFields.objects.filter(amount1__gt=F('amount2')).query)
... WHERE ("testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount1" > ("testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount2") AND "testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount1_currency" = ("testapp_modelwithtwomoneyfields"."amount2_currency"))
```
On Django versions prior to 1.8 queries are logically equal.
I'll create new PR for this issue after #165 will be merged to master (I hope it will :))

issue_title: Append instead of replace parameter cell
issue_body: Cells overwrite the contents of the parameter tagged cell which is non-intuitive and easy to mess up. Instead it would be more reasonable if the tagged cell used values there as default values and simply appended its contents to the cell definition. Repeated names would still be overwritten and non-papermill execution could enumerate sane values without breaking papermill execution as it is today. But, it would allow for easier assignment of defaults without adding a new cell. Today all the notebooks I've seen for papermill leave a blank cell for parameters which has to be explained to each person who sees a papermill notebook as opposed to a normal notebook when there shouldn't be so much of a difference.

issue_title: Satisfiability bug with CompositeFrontend
issue_body:
Hello !

I have found a sequence of step that makes the CompositeFrontend return invalid satisfiability.

I will just drop you the script to reproduce the issue :

```
import claripy

full_frontend = claripy.FullFrontend(claripy.backends.z3)
s =  claripy.CompositeFrontend(full_frontend)

#s = claripy.Solver()

c = claripy.BVS('test', 32)
s.add(c[7:0] != 0)

print(s.satisfiable())

s.add(c == 0)

s.satisfiable(extra_constraints=[claripy.BVS('lol', 32)==0])

print(s.satisfiable())
```

You can see that at the end, the constraints are clearly not satisfiable (c must be null but not null), but my satisfiable() call with extra_constraints containing another variable seems to confuse the CompositeFrontend.

If you remove that satisfiable(extra_constraints=...), or if you use the default claripy.Solver(), the bug will not appear.

Thanks :)

issue_title: Vehicle.flush() not working as intended
issue_body:
There are two issues with the flush method that I have found:

1. When I add waypoints and call flush(), the entire mission requires about .25 seconds per waypoint to write in lab conditions. If I attempt to set the vehicle to auto or download the waypoints before all of them are written, the Pixhawk will not receive the full mission and 're-request' the waypoints it missed. When flying, this caused the quad to crash since it got set to auto without home being written properly and came down. 
To get around this issue, I call time.sleep(vehicle.commands.count/2) immediately after calling vehicle.flush(). However, if flush() worked properly it would automatically wait until receiving confirmation from the quad that all waypoints had been written before continuing, much like wait_valid() does. 

2. After downloading waypoints from the Pixhawk with vehicle.commands.download() and vehicle.commands.wait_valid(), the Pixhawk no longer has a loaded mission, meaning that I can't set it to auto while armed, etc. To reupload the mission I have to actually change a command in the CommandSequence. 
I would have figured that simply calling flush() would handle the reupload without me having to change anything, but it doesn't.

These are important issues which need to be solved before lots of people start using Dronekit in real applications. Either fix the flush method add new methods to achieve the required functionality.

issue_title: Documentation example for TOTP is misleading
issue_body:
Quoting usage documentation:
```
totp = pyotp.TOTP('base32secret3232')
totp.now() # => '492039'

# OTP verified for current time
totp.verify('492039') # => True
time.sleep(30)
totp.verify('492039') # => False
```

This implies that password is valid for 30 seconds after its generation which is not true. When verifying the password this way, it's valid only until the time interval for which it was generated ends.

I suggest to either explicitly state this or change the example to include `valid_window` option.

issue_title: Swagger spec still invalid
issue_body:
The Swagger spec is still invalid it seems

Extract:

```json
/{prefix}/{api_ver:\\d+}/{application_guid}/{application_ver}/{metrics:.*}": {
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "prefix",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "api_ver:\\d+",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "application_guid",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "application_ver",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "metrics:.*",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        }
      ],
```

In this API definitions, smwogger will choke on api_ver and metrics because their definitions in the parameter list should not include the regexp and just be the name of the parameter

The right definition should be

```json
/{prefix}/{api_ver:\\d+}/{application_guid}/{application_ver}/{metrics:.*}": {
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "prefix",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "api_ver",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "application_guid",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "application_ver",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        },
        {
          "name": "metrics",
          "type": "string",
          "required": true,
          "in": "path"
        }
      ],
```

To validate that it works, you can try this small program with Smowgger to print all operations:

```python
import asyncio
from smwogger import API


async def print_operations():
    async with API('http://path.to.kinto/v1/__api__') as api:
        print(api.operations)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
try:
    loop.run_until_complete(print_operations())
finally:
    loop.close()
```


cc @gabisurita @chartjes

issue_title: Doxyfile INPUT too broad
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | N/A | 
| Firmware Version | N/A  | 
| Operating System & Version | Ubuntu 16.04  |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.4.0 |

In the doxy configuration file "doc/doxygen/doxyfile" you have the following configuration: 

```
INPUT                  = ../../include \
                         ../../include/librealsense \
                         ../ \
                         ../../src
```

In the file "doc/readme.md", to build the documentation it says to run: 
```
sudo apt-get install doxygen
doxygen doc/doxygen/doxyfile
```

It is not explicitly stated, but it seems the assumption would be to run this command from the root project directory. If you do this, the INPUT definitions point to the parent directory of the root project directory, bringing in anything found there as well. So if you have other opensource projects in that directory, the doxygen documentation also brings in things that are completely unrelated.

So instead, I ran from the "doc" subdirectory of the project. This seems to correctly find the sources for building the project. The only INPUT definition that actually matches is the '../' definition. Unfortunately, if you have patched the kernel prior to running this command, then the kernel sources will be in subdirectory of the project ("ubuntu-xenial-master" in my case). Doxygen then attempts to build all of the documentation for the entirety of the kernel sources. This quickly caused the small instance I was using to run out of memory. It seems unlikely to me that you would intentionally want to build doxygen for the entirety of the linux kernel.

I think the INPUT definition needs to be changed to avoid building the kernel's docs.

issue_title: Several issues with deepq serialization
issue_body:
I found several issues with deepq model serialization.

1. `ActWrapper.load_act` is a static method, but has `self` in its parameterlist, which causes the first argument to be ignored and makes that the `deepq.load_act` convenience function doesn't work. Changing the signature by removing the `self` argument solves this issue.

2. The environment is included in the pickle, causing cloudpickle to crash. https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/deepq/deepq.py#L197 already attempts to solve this, but in my installation the env is still captured in the closure. Replacing the function with the following solves this issue:
```
observation_space = env.observation_space
def make_obs_ph(name):
    return ObservationInput(observation_space, name=name)
```
3. The `model.save` call in the run script does not use the convenient pickler. It would be nice if `ActWrapper.save` could be replace with something like:
```
    def save(self, path):
        save_state(path)
        self.save_act(path+".pickle")
```
This way a pickled policy is immediately usable after training.

Shall I file a PR?

issue_title: SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK change in django-debug-toolbar 1.8 breaks existing code and it is not documented
issue_body:
In django-debug-toolbar 1.7 and below, the `SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK` callback is used to check both whether a request  to  debug-toolbar views should be authorized and whether a request is ajax.

In django-debug-toolbar 1.8, the ajax check is moved into the middleware, and it raises a 404 if a request can not pass the SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK check.

So a callback written for django-debug-toolbar<=1.7 will broken debug-toolbar 1.8's ajax request. :-(

Debug-toolbar's own doc still indicates that the callback should check if the request is AJAX, it's confusing：

> The default checks are that DEBUG must be set to True, the IP of the request must be in INTERNAL_IPS, and the request must not be an AJAX request. 

Because those 404s do not have explicit error messages, it is even harder to find the root cause.

issue_title: Support default t build_type
issue_body:
Hi!

To save CI time, I have avoided to build Debug packages. They are rarely used and spend precious time in our CI (travis, appveyor, gitlab). To avoid such build, I have used:

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager()
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    filtered_builds = []
    for settings, options, env_vars, build_requires in builder.builds:
        if settings["build_type"] == "Release":
             filtered_builds.append([settings, options, env_vars, build_requires])
    builder.builds = filtered_builds
    builder.run()
```

or 

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager(args="-s build_type=Release")
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    builder.run()
```

Would be great if [ConanMultiPackager](https://github.com/conan-io/conan-package-tools/blob/develop/conan/packager.py#L70) support an argument to choice only a build type:

```python
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager(build_type="Release")
    builder.add_common_builds(shared_option="foo:shared")
    builder.run()
```

Also, such feature could be used as environment variable, as `CONAN_BUILD_TYPE`, accepting only one  type. e.g. `CONAN_BUILD_TYPE=Release`

Regards.

`csvcut -c 1,2 MOCK_DATA_MALE.csv | csvsql -i postgresql --tables first_name`

I get:

```
CREATE TABLE first_name (
        id DECIMAL NOT NULL, 
        name_first VARCHAR(11) NOT NULL
);
```

When I do:

`csvcut -c 1,2 MOCK_DATA_MALE.csv | csvsql --db postgresql://aklaver:@localhost/test --tables first_name --insert`

I get:

```
postgres@test=# \d first_name 
         Table "public.first_name"
   Column   |       Type        | Modifiers 
------------+-------------------+-----------
 id         | numeric           | 
 name_first | character varying | 
```

Looks like --db is using --no-constraints  by default.
Is that what is supposed to happen?

[pair my_contacts]

issue_title: Investigate usage of packagecloud.io for Debian/Ubuntu repositories
issue_body:
Debian has policies that prevent users from using the latest vdirsyncer version. We should make it possible for users to add a repository that contains the latest version while still using their *native* package manager for updates.

The two options for this are:
* PPAs
* http://packagecloud.io

An ideal solution would automatically generate apt packages from the PyPI releases.

@hobarrera investigated this sometime ago, however it's unclear what the conclusions/results were. We should try this out again and perhaps contact packagecloud's support about our usecase. We should also clearly document what we found out.

issue_title: Error about being unable to staple a must staple cert
issue_body: If the user requests a cert with `--must-staple`, is asking `certbot` to install it, and the installer doesn't support OCSP-stapling, we should error out before requesting the cert with a helpful error message.

issue_title: Inject username of user running senza init as superuser in roles zalandos
census notebook 2 broken at head with BQ error in analyze
issue_body:
steps to reproduce:
1) build datalab from datalab/containers/datalab/build.sh <pydatalab>
2) datalab and pydatalab repros are at master
3) go to the census regression sample. Run notebook 1. Then run notebook 2. the call to analyze fails.

I extracted the code that fails, and there is some kind of problem with BQ. Note that this error does NOT show up if you run the sample from the released version (datalab create <name>)

```
import google.datalab.bigquery as bq
import json
sql = 'SELECT  max(WAGP) as max_WAGP, min(WAGP) as min_WAGP, avg(WAGP) as avg_WAGP  from csv_table'
with open('/content/datalab/workspace/census/data/schema.json', 'r') as f:
  schema_list = json.loads(f.read())
print('data path', train_data_path)
print('schema', schema_list)
table = bq.ExternalDataSource(

issue_title: Deferred status transition with Lpar.activate() and deactivate()
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587821, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20feature', 'name': 'type: feature', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
When the `Lpar.activate()` and `deactivate()` methods complete (e.g. by returning after being called with `wait_for_completion=True`), the LPAR state may not have reached the desired state, and a subsequent status retrieval still reports the old state.

This does not happen always, and the exact circumstances are not fully understood at this point.

As a result, users of the zhmcclient have to code around this and loop on status retrieval until the desired state is reached.

The zhmcclient could take care of that by performing the stats polling inside of these methods for the `wait_for_completion=True` case, making life easier for the user. In the `wait_for_completion=False` case, adressing this is more complex, but not impossible, given that we have PR #182: The async method could return a new subclass of `Job` that implements its check and wait methods such that completion includes polling for the desired status.

Discussion points:
* Should we do this at all?
* Should a timeout be added? (see Issue #6)

issue_title: Auto-resolve addresses in socket methods
issue_body:
Currently, one of the awkward differences between `socket` and `trio.socket` – really the last awkward difference as of v0.2.0 – is that with `socket`, you can pass hostnames directly to socket methods, like `sock.bind(("localhost", 0))` or `sock.connect(("python.org", 443))`. In Trio these methods currently require numeric addresses, and there are the extra methods `resolve_local_address` and `resolve_remote_address` that do the resolution for you.

This was always a little weird, and really the only compelling reason was because `bind` as synchronous. But it turns out that `bind` should be async after all (#241). So we might as well get rid of this last piece of weirdness.

Plan:

* Make `bind`, `connect`, etc. all call `resolve_local_address`/`resolve_remote_address` automatically as needed
* Deprecate `resolve_local_address`/`resolve_remote_address`

issue_title: control-C sometimes getting missed on Windows
issue_body:
We're getting occasional failures on appveyor, that look like:

```
\trio\_core\tests\test_ki.py::test_ki_is_good_neighbor PASSED
..\trio\_core\tests\test_ki.py::test_ki_wakes_us_up 
[... coverage info ...]

([source](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/njsmith/trio/build/1.0.271/job/fucboe5llmwcn0eu))

This is a test that delivers a synthetic SIGINT while the trio thread is sleeping, to make sure that the `KeyboardInterrupt` gets delivered promptly.

`_run.py:1136` is the `raise KeyboardInterrupt` at the end of `run()` – the one that checks `ki.pending` one last time as `run` is exiting.

Given the time report at the end, it looks like the `await sleep(20)` in `test_ki_wakes_us_up` may be expiring? And that's the only checkpoint in the test, so if we somehow aren't being woken up by the signal arriving, but the signal is in fact arriving, then this would make sense.

issue_title: getCookie function matches on partial matches
issue_body:
The function getCookies https://github.com/neutronX/django-markdownx/blob/de43019c84b5ae0512c89c7f8d9d08cdfd3b9393/markdownx/static/markdownx/js/markdownx.js#L589 reads out the cookies and then tries to match them against the given name. The function however matches ```name+'='``` anywhere in the string so that also ```subname + name + '='``` will be matched, causing the wrong cookie to be returned.

The function is used to get the 'csrftoken' cookie. The result of an incorrect lookup means that the csrf token will be rejected by the server.

An easy fix is to change the following line:
```
-              .filter(cookie => cookie.indexOf(`${name}=`) !== -1)[0];
+              .filter(cookie => cookie.trim().indexOf(`${name}=`) == 0)[0];
```

Would it be possible to fix this?

issue_title: Check matching shapes in model-checker/shape_inference
issue_body:
I think (at least) the VGG19 example onnx file has an error which is not detected due to insufficient checking of input/output dimensions.

It affects the first GEMM layer. The output of the preceding MaxPool layer is: 
`"pool5_1"{ tensor:FLOAT, shape: [1x512x7x7]}`

The GEMM layer gets `[u'pool5_1', u'fc6_w_0', u'fc6_b_0'])` with `%fc6_w_0[FLOAT, 4096x25088]
  %fc6_b_0[FLOAT, 4096]`

While this is practically matching (`512*7*7 == 25088`) it would need an extra Flatten layer to be valid because Gemm is defined as A[M x K] * B[K x N].

I therefore propose to add appropriate checks to the model-checker or at least to the shape_inference

issue_title: [Feature Request] - Verbose option for the Layer API
issue_body:
Hello dear friends,

I have used TL for quite a while and I'm really thankful for all the amazing work.

One thing which appeared very cool to me from the beginning was the verbose graph definition which helped me a lot during debug and development. However, I figured out a bit later that are no way to deactivate the followings:

```shell
  [TL] InputLayer  encoder/input: (?, 256, 256, 1)
  [TL] Conv2dLayer encoder/h1/conv2d: shape:[5, 5, 1, 64] strides:[1, 2, 2, 1] pad:VALID act:identity
  [TL] BatchNormLayer encoder/h1/batch_norm: decay:0.900000 epsilon:0.000010 act:identity is_train:True
```

It is a really interesting feature to have these information, however it could rapidly clutter the console output. 

#### Feature Request

The idea would be to make this output optional (default = True or False). I think there could be different ways to do this.

##### 1. Solution - Create a **verbose** parameter in the Layer API
Simple and backward compatible, a "verbose" parameter can be added to the Layer Class and influence the behavior of [print_params()](https://github.com/tensorlayer/tensorlayer/blob/master/tensorlayer/layers.py#L321) method.

##### 2. Use the Logging module from TF.

Why should we re-invent the wheel ? Everything is already implemented in Tensorflow.
We can use the logging level already existing in TF.

```python
tf.logging._level_names    ## outputs => {50: 'FATAL', 40: 'ERROR', 30: 'WARN', 20: 'INFO', 10: 'DEBUG'}
tf.logging.get_verbosity() ## outputs => 30 (default value)

tf.logging.set_verbosity(tf.logging.DEBUG)
tf.logging.get_verbosity() ## outputs => 10
```

We could for instance determine that for logging level <= 20 (INFO & DEBUG), we normally output the Tensorlayer informations, and we don't perform this action for any higher value.


All the best,

Jonathan

issue_title: createdb `database` table / `database_ID` column
issue_body:
The database table of the SQLite as it is currently created makes it somewhat difficult to read and edit dataset metadata.

Also, the dataset_ID column in other tables insinuates that it might be possible to have multiple datasets in one file in the future, but that would clearly require a primary key structure where dataset_ID is part of the primary key. If we want to support cases of multiple datasets in one file in the future, let's make that change now before I and who knows else starts assuming that that column can be safely ignored; If we never want to have that multiple datasets in one file, remove that column.
